summary: My name is Grace Abbott, born October 20th, 1907 in Sebastopol, California. I currently live at 445 Twin Lakes Circle in Oakmont Village, Santa Rosa. I have deep family roots here, with my grandparents having settled in Sonoma County during the mid-1800s. I cherish the old family photographs and stories passed down about my ancestors' journeys and lives in this region.

I live in Santa Rosa, specifically in Oakmont Village at 445 Twin Lakes Circle. My parents were married in Santa Rosa in 1903, and I have a treasured wedding photo of them taken there. Santa Rosa has been central to many family events and memories throughout my life.

Oakmont Village in Santa Rosa is where I currently reside. It’s a peaceful place that connects me to the community and my family history in Sonoma County. Living here keeps me close to the places my family has called home for generations.

I was born in Sebastopol at my grandmother’s house on Florence Avenue in 1907. My mother’s family lived in town there, and it’s where my parents likely met. Sebastopol has always been a significant place in my family’s story.

Healdsburg is near where my grandmother’s house on Florence Avenue is located, though our house was closer to Bodega. It’s part of the broader area where my family settled and lived in Sonoma County. I often think about the connections between these small towns in our family history.

My grandmother’s home where I was born was near Bodega, just a few houses away from the Strout House on Florence Avenue. Bodega is part of the rural area where my family lived and farmed. It holds a special place in my memories of childhood and family roots.

The Strout House is a well-known old house near where I was born on Florence Avenue. One of the Strout daughters, Erma, is an aunt by marriage to me, so I’m quite familiar with the family and the house. It’s a landmark in our family’s local history.

My grandfather Winkler came from Kentucky when he was sixteen in 1849, drawn by the California Gold Rush. Although he initially went for gold, he soon decided to farm instead, which suited him better as a farmboy. Kentucky is where our family’s journey to California began.

The California Gold Rush of 1849 was the reason my grandfather Winkler left Kentucky and came west. He was just sixteen and initially aimed to find gold, but he chose farming over mining once he arrived. This event was a pivotal moment that brought my family to California.

My maternal grandfather came from Missouri sometime in the mid-1800s before settling in California. My mother was born there in 1879, along with most of her siblings, before the family moved west via the Oregon Trail. Missouri was the starting point for my mother’s side of the family’s journey.

My mother’s family came down from Oregon to California after my grandfather first settled here. They traveled the northern route across the United States, and Oregon was part of that journey. It was a time when the west was still being settled and life was challenging but hopeful.

My mother’s family traveled the Oregon Trail when they moved from Missouri to California. Though I don’t recall many details, my mother was old enough to remember being happy to leave Missouri behind. The Oregon Trail was a tough but important route for our family’s migration westward.

My grandfather Winkler came over the Sierras and into Sacramento before settling down in Sonoma County. Sacramento was a key point on his journey west with a team of oxen. It represents an important stop in the migration story of my father’s family.

Sonoma County is where my family settled after traveling west in the mid-1800s. My father’s family farmed in Green Valley near Graton, and I grew up in this area. It’s been home to several generations of my family, rich with agricultural history and local community ties.

During my grandfather Winkler’s journey west, he had some minor skirmishes with local Indians, mostly involving stolen horses. Thankfully, these encounters were not serious and the Indians were more interested in cattle than causing harm. These stories are part of the early pioneer experience in our family history.

My maternal grandfather was a Civil War veteran who served as a drummer and water boy for the Union Army. Though he lived in the South, his family were Northern sympathizers and he escaped conscription into the Confederate Army by fleeing north. He rarely spoke of the war, but I have some letters from him dating to 1918 that mention his experiences.

My grandfather served in the Union Army during the Civil War as a drummer boy and water carrier. Though he was young and from the South, his family supported the North. His service was a significant part of his life story, even if he didn’t talk about it much.

My grandfather avoided conscription into the Confederate States Army by escaping through the underground to join his father in the North. His family sympathized with the Union, so this was a dangerous but necessary move. This part of his story highlights the divided loyalties during the Civil War.

My father’s family lived and farmed in Green Valley, just beyond Graton. I remember living on a small ranch west of Graton where my dad built the barn first and we lived there until the house was finished. Green Valley is part of the rural landscape that shaped my childhood and family life.

I lived in Graton from about age five until I married, on a small ranch where my father farmed cherries, grapes, and Gravenstein apples. My mother was active in the Graton Women's Club, and the community was close-knit and rural. Graton holds many of my fondest childhood memories.

Santa Rosa is where I currently live and where my parents were married in 1903. It’s a central place in my family’s history and where many important family events took place. I have cherished photographs from Santa Rosa that capture those moments.

My father planted Gravenstein apples in the vineyards after pulling out grapes, so the orchard would be productive. The Gravenstein apple was an important crop on our family farm near Graton. It symbolized the agricultural life we led and the cycles of farming in Sonoma County.

Our first car was a Ford, which was quite simple to operate. My mother learned to drive it despite my father's initial reluctance, and I remember riding in the back seat as she drove. The Ford was an important part of our family’s transportation and independence in rural California.
The transcript does not mention Marshall School, so I have no personal stories or connections to share about it.
Oak Grove is not directly mentioned in my family stories or personal history, so I do not have specific memories or connections to that place.
The 1906 San Francisco earthquake is not something I discussed or have direct memories of in my family stories. It may have affected the region, but it was not a focus in my recollections.

San Francisco is near our region, but my family’s stories and life mostly centered around Sonoma County and smaller towns like Sebastopol and Santa Rosa. I don’t have detailed personal connections to San Francisco itself.

My mother, Charlotte, was a member of the Graton Women's Club, which was an important part of her social life. The club still operates today and hosts events like a spring garden show. It represents the community involvement and social fabric of our rural town.
